title: Syndicated Lending, Competition, and Relative Performance Evaluation
abstract: Relative performance evaluation (RPE) intensifies competitive pressure by tying executive compensation to the profits of rivals. We show that these contracts make loan syndication harder by reducing banks’ willingness to participate in loans underwritten by banks named in their RPE contracts. Lead arranger banks, which are more frequently named in RPE, hold larger shares of the loans they syndicate, and their borrowers receive smaller and fewer loans and face higher spreads. Our results highlight the tension between the normal benefits of competition versus the need for cooperation in loan syndication.

article.1 Setting:
JEL G21 - Banks; Depository Institutions; Micro Finance Institutions; Mortgages G24 - Investment Banking; Venture Capital; Brokerage; Ratings and Ratings Agencies G30 - General M12 - Personnel Management; Executives; Executive Compensation Issue Section: Article

Syndicated lending requires cooperation between lead arrangers (typically banks) and investors (often, other banks). The cooperative nature of this market makes it unusual in banking. This paper shows that increases in the intensity of competition, generated by relative performance evaluation contracts (RPE), increase the cost of arranging syndicated loans.1

Lead arrangers underwrite syndicated loans, although unlike securities underwriters they typically retain some of these loans themselves. Banks compete for lead arranger status because winning the so-called “mandate” brings fees as well as prestige (“League table status”). After winning the mandate, leads sell participation to other bank and nonbank investors. Higher demand from investors leads to better terms for both the borrower and the lead arranger: lower spreads, looser nonprice terms, and a lower share held by the lead bank (Bruche, Malherbe, and Meisenzahl 2020). Many investors in syndicated loans are other banks, especially in revolving lines of credit where banks dominate (Kashyap, Rajan, and Stein 2022;Gatev and Strahan 2006). RPE thus creates tension for investor banks. Investing in a given loan helps the lead arranger succeed in its underwriter role and thus, at the margin, raises the profits of the lead bank.

This paper shows that RPE reduces the demand for banks to invest in rivals’ syndicated loans, thereby making underwriting more difficult for lead arrangers. A common RPE contract ties bonuses of senior management to the bank’s relative standing among named, rival banks (the RPE peer group). This scheme intensifies competition and decreases the likelihood of cooperation. For example, in 2016, JPMorgan’s senior managers received performance bonuses based on the bank’s profits relative to Bank of America, Barclays, Capital One Financial, Citigroup, Credit Suisse, Deutsche Bank, Goldman Sachs, HSBC, Morgan Stanley, UBS, and Wells Fargo. As a result, JPMorgan will be less willing to invest in loans arranged by these banks than loans arranged by banks outside their RPE peer group, such as PNC or SunTrust.2

We first analyze banks’ choice to invest (“participate”) in syndicated loans arranged by other banks, focusing on the top-10 U.S. banks and top-5 foreign banks in the U.S. syndicated loan market. This set covers over 85% of the market collectively (Table 1). For each loan arranged by one of these banks, we generate 14 observations (one per potential investor). The outcome of interest, a series of zeroes and ones, depends on whether each of the candidate investors chooses to participate. We model the probability that a given bank participates in that loan as a function of whether that bank names the lead in its RPE contract. We show that the likelihood of such an investment declines by up to 9% (relative to the average) if a given bank has named the lead arranger in its RPE compensation contract. To continue the example, our regressions suggest that JPMorgan would be up to 9% less likely to participate in a loan arranged by Bank of America than one arranged by SunTrust.

The effect of RPE on a bank’s willingness to participate is stronger for lines of credit than term loans. Term loans offer a useful comparison because this market segment is easier to underwrite and has greater post-syndication liquidity (Berger, Zhang, and Zhao 2021). Potential investors in term loans include not only banks but also a large array of nonbank institutions, such as collateralized loan obligations, loan mutual funds, pension funds, hedge funds, and others. In contrast, banks have historically and continue to dominate the market for lines of credit due to their unique ability to provide liquidity on demand (Gatev and Strahan 2009;Li, Strahan, and Zhang 2020). In recent years nonbank investors held almost 60% of syndicated term loans; in contrast, banks have held more than 90% of revolving lines of credit consistently over the past three decades.3

We control not only for past relationships between potential investor-banks and the borrower but also for fixed effects at the lead-bank/investor-bank level, the potential investor-bank/time level, and the loan level (which subsumes borrower level and lead-bank/time level). Although we absorb as much unobserved heterogeneity as possible this way, these results are only suggestive (as opposed to causal) because banks decide which of their rivals to name in their RPE contracts (ie, “treatment” is not randomly assigned) and because borrowers decide which bank underwrites their loans.

Second, we address these endogeneity concerns. We aggregate to the loan level (rather than the loan-lead/participant level), and test whether the prevalence of RPE compensation schemes across the banking sector constrains investors’ demand. In this approach, we mitigate the endogeneity of RPE contracts, first by exploiting variation in RPE from the market’s point of view (rather than from the potential investor-bank’s). With this approach, we do not condition on either the lead bank’s RPE or the RPE of any of the loan participants. The idea is this: if a given lead arranger is named in the RPE contracts of a large percentage of potential investor banks, we would expect lower demand for their loans. Low demand implies higher spreads, smaller loans, and a higher lead-arranger share. We provide evidence consistent with these implications, using the variable% banks with RPE vs. lead, which equals the fraction of potential bank investors with RPE contracts naming this loan’s lead arranger. This variable measures the market demand from the top banks potentially influenced by RPE. Because% banks with RPE vs. leaddepends on the actions of banks other than the lead, it varies exogenously from the perspective of the lead arranger. Notably,% banks with RPE vs. leadexhibits substantial cross-sectional and time-series variations that have trended upward throughout our sample as the prevalence of RPE contracts has increased.

We find higher spreads and smaller loans when the underwriter faces greater% banks with RPE vs. lead.The all-in drawn spread on credit lines increases by about 3.5 basis points for a one-standard deviation increase in% banks with RPE vs. lead(6.0 basis points in 2SLS, discussed below). And loan size decreases by about 6.5% for a one-sigma rise in% banks with RPE vs. lead(8.6% in 2SLS). Consistent with our results on participation, we find no statistically significant effect on price (spreads) or quantity (loan size) for term loans. Because lead arrangers are not constrained to sell investments in term loans to other banks—they more often sell to nonbanks—RPE contracts do not affect loan terms in this segment. We also test, and find, evidence that other terms of credit lines adjust with% banks with RPE vs. lead.

To further establish causality, we build instruments for% banks with RPE vs. lead, which do not depend on the borrower’s choice of lead. The instruments exploit three facts:(1)bank-borrower relationships are sticky;(2)lead arrangers specialize across industry; and(3)lead banks are geographically concentrated. The first instrument,% banks with RPE vs. past leads, is a weighted average of% banks with RPE vs. leadacross the bank(s) that acted as lead arranger on a given borrower’s past loans. The second instrument,% banks with RPE vs. industry leads, averages across all loans made in prior years to all firms in the borrower’s four-digit SIC industry. The third,% banks with RPE vs. state leads, averages across all loans made in prior years to firms in the borrower’s headquarters state. All three instruments are strongly related to% banks with RPE vs. leadin the first stage (both individually and collectively), and all three are constructed without regard to the actual lead arranger selected on the loan. The estimated effects on both loan size and spreads are larger (although of the same order of magnitude) than in the base-case ordinary least squares (OLS)/fixed effects framework, helping allay any residual concerns about endogeneity.

Third, we test whether lead-lender RPE constrains borrower credit by modeling which Compustat firms receive bank loans reported in DealScan. We continue with the logic of our instruments, but reduce the dimensionality by using the first principal component, which is close to a simple average of our three instruments, as each gets a positive and similar-sized factor loading. This measure decreases the probability of getting a loan, conditional on time and firm fixed effects as well as time-varying firm characteristics. And the effects are concentrated in credit lines. We infer from the results that borrowers are worse off if their most “natural” lead lender—a past lender or a lender with specialization in their industry or their locality—is named frequently in RPEs of potential investor banks.

Finally, we examine the effects of% banks with RPE vs. leadfrom the lead arranger’s perspective. Stronger competition and weaker loan demand from rival banks ought to lower market share for banks most exposed to RPE. Consistent with this idea, we verify that the adoption of RPE contracts has lowered lead-bank market share. The effect represents the relative effect of RPE on banks more versus less exposed. We find that a one-sigma increase in% banks with RPE vs. leadlowers market share by about half of a percentage point, or around 12% relative to the sample mean.

Our paper extends the banking and compensation literatures by exposing a downside of RPE contracts in forming loan syndicates. RPE contracts have become increasingly prevalent perhaps in response to shareholders’ attempts to limit “pay for luck” (seeBertrand and Mullainathan 2001;Garvey and Milbourn 2006;Jenter and Kanaan 2015, among others).4Although the prior literature has studied competition and RPE in a variety of economic contexts (for an excellent survey, seeChowdhury and Gurtler 2015), we are among the first to provide direct evidence of the competitive frictions caused by RPE for bank lending.5We complementFeichter, Moers, and Timmermans (2022), who find that RPE inspires firms to take more aggressive and more complex competitive actions, such as introducing new products and launching new marketing campaigns, andBloomfield, Heinle, and Timmermans (2023), who find that firms routinely disclose information that harms RPE peers’ stock prices.6

We also contribute to papers linking executive pay or effort by lending officers to syndicated lending outcomes (Herpfer 2021;Gao, Karolyi, and Pacelli 2018). We extend this literature by emphasizing the tension between competition and cooperation, which shapes the underwriting process because lead banks face the challenge of selling participations to their competitors (ie, other banks). The compensation of those competitors matters to the extent that these contracts change their willingness to cooperate. Our results suggest that this underwriting function becomes more difficult when relative performance contracts enhance competition or rivalry between banks.
1.1 Syndicated lending

Our sample construction starts with all U.S. dollar-denominated syndicated loan deals originated in the United States in the Thomson-Reuters LPC DealScan database from 2010 to 2019. This decade represents a relatively mature period for the U.S. syndicated loan market, abutted by periods of volatility pertaining to the global financial crisis of 2008 and the coronavirus pandemic beginning in 2020. DealScan provides detailed information on syndicated loan originations, including the names and roles of all lenders involved in each deal. We map DealScan lenders to bank holding companies using the 2020 vintage of the DealScan Lender Link table curated by Michael Schwert and described inSchwert (2018).7In tests requiring data on borrower-firm characteristics, we map borrowers to Compustat using the 2018 release of the linking table fromChava and Roberts (2008).8

We classify each syndicate’s lenders into primary lead arrangers, secondary colead arrangers, and nonlead participants beginning with the procedure outlined byIvashina (2009). When available, we assign theAdministrative agentas the primary lead arranger. The administrative agent conducts due diligence and monitors the borrower, handles loan payments, and typically retains the largest economic stake. If the syndicate does not have an administrative agent, we followBharath et al. (2011)and consider other roles that receive lead credit for purposes of league tables as available in DealScan.9These schemata identify primary lead arrangers for 94% and 3% of sample loans, respectively. We further classify all lenders who receive lead credit for league table status but are not primary lead arrangers as secondary colead arrangers. This second tier of lenders often have preexisting relationships with the borrower, commit more capital than other participants, and play some active role in the underwriting process, but they do not bear the same level of syndication risk as the deal’s primary lead arranger (S&P 2016). Colead roles are typically afforded a share of fees and gain intangible benefits including prestige and relationship access to the borrower (S&P 2016).10The remaining lenders are nonlead participants. Since our objective is to study the costs of arranging syndicated loans induced by RPE contracts, we require that each syndicate have a single primary lead arranger, which constitutes 97% of syndicates. This restriction allows us to construct intuitive pairwise measures between the lead bank and potential syndicate members.

We focus on the top-10 U.S. banks and top-5 foreign banks. These banks are active players both as lead arrangers, colead arrangers, and participants.Table 1shows the market share of the sample banks by lender role and loan type (seeTable A.1for variable definitions). As lead arrangers, their lending accounts for over 85% of the U.S. syndicated loan market by dollar volume. These lead banks’ dominance is even greater for credit lines, where they represent over 90% of the market. These banks also play a substantial role as coleads and participants. For example, Bank of America is a colead on over 40% of credit lines, while U.S. Bancorp is a participant on over 35% of credit lines. These banks typically reference each other in their RPE contracts, making other smaller banks less relevant for our study.

1.2 RPE contracts

We obtain data on RPE in executive compensation from Institutional Shareholder Services’ Incentive Lab. Incentive Lab contains hand-collected items from mandatory SEC filings, including data on the underlying reference groups used to determine payouts from performance-contingent compensation awards granted to named executive officers at public U.S. firms.11Since Incentive Lab only covers U.S. firms, we complete our sample by hand collecting data on RPE by foreign banks from their annual disclosures.

Table 2shows annual RPE use by our sample banks. We code banks that benchmark their executives’ RPE awards against a peer group as “Yes.” We code banks as “No” if they do not use RPE awards, if they subjectively use relative comparisons to determine pay without any formulaic benchmarking, or if they benchmark their RPE awards against a broad index, such as the Dow Jones U.S. Banks Index or S&P 500 Financials Index.12As this table shows, RPE has become more prevalent over the course of our sample period, rising from about one-third of sample banks in 2010 to over two-thirds in 2019.

These contracts discourage investment in loans arranged by banks named in a potential investor’s RPE peer group. As explained inBruche, Malherbe, and Meisenzahl (2020), lead arrangers bear “pipeline” risk in syndication because they offer borrowers a firm commitment, which insures the borrower against weak demand for their loan. In their example, a lead arranger would receive an upfront fee of 2% to syndicate a $100 million loan. However, if demand were insufficient to syndicate the full $100 million at the lead’s first-round proposed interest spread (LIBOR+450 basis points in their example), the rate could flex upward to draw additional interest from investors. Such flexing would be costly to the lead bank (as well as to the borrower) because the upfront fee would simultaneously decline. In their example, the lead arranger’s fee would be negative (a loss) if demand were sufficiently low. In these cases, weak demand also requires the lead to hold a larger share of the loan. Hence, a potential investor bank who names the lead as an RPE peer knows that nonparticipation is potentially very harmful to the lead arranger (by lowering demand); their profit from investing in the loan would have to more than offset the gain to the lead arranger from stronger investor demand. Finishing the example, under strong demand, the arranger would receive $2 million in fees; if $75 million of the loan were syndicated out (with the lead committing the other $25 million), this would translate into an ROA from the fees of 8% (=$2 million/$25 million × 100), or an ROE of something like 80% (assuming 10-1 leverage for the lead). Under low demand, fees income could go as low as –$0.25 million.

The nature of this trade-off—that is, whether the benefit of investing outweighs the cost of helping an RPE rival—depends on a bank’s role in the syndicate. As described above,coleadsstand to gain more both financially and reputation-wise as they often receive a share of upfront fees, get credit in league tables, and develop relationships with the borrower, whileparticipantsare afforded fewer benefits, lower fees (or no fees), and only gain from the return on the capital they commit.13Potential investor-banks that name a deal’s primary lead arranger in their RPE contracts face an unpleasant trade-off. On one hand, they are rewarded in accordance with their respective syndicate roles. Offsetting these gains, investing helps the lead arranger in successfully syndicating the deal, thereby increasing the lead’s reputation and profits, and making it more difficult to outperform the lead in RPE. We hypothesize that the effect of RPE on investment will be more pronounced forparticipantroles as the opportunity cost of forgoing investment in any specific loan is low.

Importantly, we do not suggest that RPE contracts are value-destroying from the perspective of the firm itself. Naming banks in my RPE does not affect my ability to syndicate loans. If it did, this would raise questions about why firms award them in the first place.14Instead, the behavior ofotherfirms’ use of RPE can raise the cost of cooperation. So, there may be a collective action problem in which the overall industry is harmed by the widespread use of RPE, even though each firm’s decision to implement these contracts is individually optimal.

When discussing the reasons behind the adoption or removal of RPE, banks in our sample typically cite pressure from shareholders. For example, when Citigroup adopted RPE in 2013, they explained, “After meeting with nearly a third of our common share ownership, as well as independent proxy advisory firms, some common themes emerged […] In sum, you asked for an improved connection between pay and performance.” And that, “Relative total shareholder return was identified as a preferred long-term metric.”15However, when determining Citi’s 2016 compensation, they stated that, “Although last year’s Performance Share Units were based solely on relative total shareholder return, we moved away from the metric this year because, during our stockholder outreach efforts, we heard little support for retaining a standalone performance metric based on total shareholder return. Nearly all investors said that they preferred operational metrics in an incentive pay program because operational results are more directly influenced by management’s decisions than total shareholder returns.” These disclosures suggest that banks’ decision to use RPE is primarily for reasons spanning beyond the syndicated loan market.16However, since the choice of RPE peers is endogenous, the core of our analysis employs instrumental variables estimation (described below).

The aggregate uptrend in RPE adoptions among our sample banks continues the trend from the prior decade documented byBloomfield, Marvao, and Spagnolo (2023)at large international banks and mirrors the aggregate trend in RPE use among S&P 1500 firms documented byBizjak et al. (2022). We also visualize the time-series variation of RPE use in our sample by plotting the fraction of potential syndicate members that benchmark RPE awards against the deal’s lead arranger, weighted by the number of deals. AsFigure 1shows, this fraction has increased from approximately 25% in 2010 to almost 40% in 2019.

Figure 1also shows, however, a substantial cross-sectional heterogeneity in RPE use even toward the end of our sample.Figure A.1provides a detailed breakdown of the number of times each sample bank is referenced as an RPE peer in 2018. For instance, Bank of America is cited as an RPE peer by eight of the other sample banks, while Goldman Sachs is cited only twice. We conclude that our empirical setting contains sufficient time-series and cross-sectional variation.

In our sample, the average (median) bank lists 10.4 (11) competitors in its peer group. Banks at the 10th percentile list only five peers, while those at the 90th percentile list 12. These peer groups are relatively small compared to those documented in prior studies, including the average peer group size of 16.6 reported byBakke, Mahmudi, and Newton (2020). This is plausibly because of the concentrated nature of the banking industry compared to other industries. Small peer group sizes mean that bankers stand to gain more with each peer they manage to outperform, as RPE contracts are usually in the form of rank-order tournaments.17

To illustrate the form of a typical RPE contract disclosure,Appendix Bpresents excerpts from the Compensation Discussion and Analysis sections of JPMorgan’s annual proxy statements. In 2015, JPM initiated a 3-year performance share unit (PSU) program for all their named executive officers, including CEO James Dimon.18The fair value of the PSU award accounted for over 75% of the value of Mr Dimon’s total compensation, representing a significant economic stake.19The award vested equally in thirds over the next 3 years depending on both absolute and relative return on tangible common equity (ROTCE) performance. The award formula was designed to payout nothing for absolute ROTCE below 6% and payout 150% of the target value for absolute ROTCE above 14%. For ROTCE falling between these thresholds and maximum values, the payout formula was based on relative ROTCE compared to JPM’s peer group. Depending on JPM’s percentile ranking within the peer group, the award payout ranged between 25% and 150% of the target value. Over much of the range, moving up by one rank would increase Mr Dimon’s expected annual payout by approximately one million dollars.20Thus, we argue that these relative compensation awards can induce strong monetary incentives to compete with RPE peers. The details of performance metrics vary substantially across RPE contracts, so our empirical design focuses only on whether a given bank is named in the RPE contract; this simple metric provides comparability across contracts.
article.2 Empirical Design:
As we have argued, RPE complicates the syndication process. To test this idea, we construct models at four levels of aggregation: syndicate level, loan level, borrower level, and lead arranger level.21

2.1 Syndicate level

Our first set of tests studies the effects of RPE on syndicate participation. We construct a data set that takes the identity of the lead arranger as given and then considers the choices made by all potential members. Sample loans generate one observation for each of our candidate investor banks, which we limit to the set of top-10 U.S. banks and top-5 foreign banks in the U.S. syndicated loan market.22The outcomeSyndicate memberdemarks whether the bank was involved in the syndicate. We further stratifySyndicate memberintoColead memberandParticipant memberbased on the bank’s role in the syndicate as described above. We define the variable of interest—RPE vs. lead arranger—as equal to one if the potential syndicate member benchmarks its RPE against the primary lead arranger.

Appendix Cillustrates the structure of the regression data using a single loan as an example. In 2011, Under Armour Inc. received a 4-year, $300 million revolving line of credit arranged by PNC Bank. As shown, seven other banks invested in the loan syndicate, two as coleads (Bank of America and SunTrust) and the other five as participants; of these seven banks, four came from the set of the top U.S. banks in our sample. In our data, this loan generates 14 observations (one for each of the sample banks other than PNC); the variableSyndicate Memberequals one for Bank of America, Sun Trust, JPMorgan, and Wells Fargo and equals zero for the remaining banks. The variablesColead memberandParticipant memberdecomposeSyndicate memberbased on each bank’s role. Here,Coleadturns to one for Bank of America and SunTrust andParticipantturns to one for JPMorgan and Wells Fargo. As such, the latter two variables add up to the first.

With this data structure, we estimate linear probability models of syndicate membership as follows: Syndicate member l , i = β RPE vs. lead i , j , t + ϕ l + ϕ i , j + ϕ i , t + ε l , i , (1) where l indexes the loan and i is the potential investor-bank. We report an analog to Equation (1) using Colead and Participant as outcomes. Additional subscripts j and t denote the identity of the lead and the year of origination. This model includes granular loan-level fixed effects, ϕ l ⁠ , which remove variation from loan, borrower-year, and lead-year characteristics. The addition of investor bank-by-lead arranger fixed effects, ϕ i , j ⁠ , absorbs constant bank-pair relationship effects. This renders the model similar to a difference-in-differences framework by requiring R P E v s . l e a d i , j , t to vary within bank-pair over time to identify β . We also include bank-year fixed effects, ϕ i , t ⁠ , to capture time trends in each potential investor-banks’ overall syndicated loan activity. To construct standard errors, we double cluster by loan and by investor bank-year. In addition to these fixed effects, we control for bank-borrower relationships using a set of dummy variables that indicate if the bank was involved in the borrower’s prior syndicated loan deals as the lead arranger, a colead, or a participant.

Our core argument implies that banks ought to be less likely to join loan syndicates if they benchmark their RPE against the lead arranger. This tendency, as we argue above, may be weaker if banks are able to obtain more prestigious or more profitable roles within the syndicate. Thus, we hypothesize that the negative relationship between RPE and membership will be stronger forparticipantroles and weaker or nonexistent forcoleadroles, as the latter are afforded league table status and may receive favorable terms on pricing.

Next, we consider syndication risk and split loans into credit lines and term loans. Credit lines oblige lenders to provide capital on demand over the life of the facility, which necessitates active liquidity management. In contrast, term loans are largely analogous to traditional bond issuances where lenders only transfer capital at origination. While the market for credit lines is dominated by banks, term loans are held by a variety of institutions and often trade in secondary markets. We hypothesize that banks’ reticence to contribute capital to their RPE peers’ syndicates is greater for credit lines and lesser for term loans because the effect of withholding capital in the term loan market is diluted by the presence of substitute lenders. Additionally, banks may join term loan syndicates to secure loan shares on behalf of nonbank clients.

2.2 Loan level

Next, we aggregate the data up to the loan level to test whether lead arrangers who are more often named in the RPE contracts of potential investors produce less favorable terms for borrowers on the loans that they syndicate. We construct the fraction of potential investors from the set of sample banks who have named the lead arranger, weighted by each of these bank’s share of loans from the previous year ( % banks with RPE vs. lead ). This measure reflects the collective RPE decisions of sample banks other than the lead arranger itself, so it varies exogenously from the perspective of the lead and does not depend on the lead arranger’s RPE contract. The model includes one observation per loan (in contrast to Equation (1) ), and has the following structure: Y l = β % banks with RPE vs. lead j , t + ϕ b + ϕ j + ϕ t + ε l . (2)

We focus on five outcomes:(1)the log of loan size (Loan amount);(2)the price of the loan to the borrower (All-in drawn spread);(3)the share of the loan retained by the lead arranger (Lead arranger share), which increases with both the lead’s risk exposure and the marginal capital requirement;(4)the fraction of lenders other than the sample of top-15 banks in the syndicate (Other lenders fraction); and (5) the number of lenders contributing capital to the syndicate (Syndicate size). As inEquation (1), the additional subscriptjkeeps track of the identity of the lead arranger,brepresents the borrowing firm, andtis the year of origination. This model includes a borrower-level fixed effect to capture firm characteristics, as well as both lead-arranger and year effects. As with the syndicate-level analysis, we split our sample to test whether the effect of% banks with RPE vs. leaddiffers between credit lines versus term loans.

To further allay endogeneity concerns, we estimate a 2SLS model with three instruments for% banks with RPE vs. lead, none of which depends on the borrower’s choice of lead arranger. The first instrument,% banks with RPE vs. past leads, is the average of% banks with RPE vs. lead, weighted by number of loans and considering all lead arrangers from whom the borrower has borrowed within the past 3 years; we start the 3-year look back from the time of the borrowers last loan.23The second and third instruments,% banks with RPE vs. industry leadsand% banks with RPE vs. state leadsare market-share weighted average across lead arrangers in the borrowers’ four-digit SIC or home state, respectively.24Each of these three instruments is uniquely defined by the borrower, not the lead arranger on a given loan. They capture how “exposed” a borrower is to lead-lender RPE based on their past banking relationships, their industry, and their location. As we show below, each has independent power to explain% banks with RPE vs. lead.

2.3 Borrower level

In our next set of tests, we look at the effects of RPE exposure from the borrower’s perspective. We build a panel data set of borrowers by combining Compustat with DealScan (and exclude Compustat firms that never appear in DealScan). To build the sample, we keep a Compustat firm in the panel going forward once it has appeared in DealScan. If they do not borrow again within a decade, we drop them. This strikes a balance between including firms that borrow infrequently (say, every 60 months or so) and excluding those who are no longer active borrowers in the syndicated loan market. 25 We estimate regressions with the following structure: New Loan b , t = β RPE exposure b , t + γ Controls b , t + ϕ b + ϕ t + ε b , t , (3) where New loan b , t represents an indicator equal to one if firm b has taken out a new loan in year t , which we identify from DealScan. RPE exposure equals the first principal component from the set of three instruments defined above ( % banks with RPE vs. past leads , % banks with RPE vs. industry leads , and % banks with RPE vs. state leads ). As shown in Appendix D , the first principal component captures about 78% of the variance across these three variables, and the factor loadings are all positive and of similar magnitude. Thus, the measure is close to a simple average across the three, standardized to have mean zero and unit variance. RPE exposure captures the average level of RPE prevailing among the large banks most likely to act as the borrower’s lead arranger. Equation (3) thus tests whether borrowers are adversely affected when their “natural” lender—based on the borrower’s past relationships, its industry, and its location—is named more frequently in RPE contracts. Since this analysis employs Compustat data, we control for standard time-varying borrower characteristics including size (log assets), profitability (return-on-assets), asset tangibility (PP&E-to-assets), and growth options (market-to-book).

2.4 Lead-arranger level

In our last set of tests, we aggregate the data up further to the level of the lead arranger-year. Lead arrangers facing more potential investors naming them in the RPE contract ought to face lower demand than other leads less likely to be named; hence, banks with higher % banks with RPE vs. lead should experience lower market shares. This essentially tests the quantity implication of low demand, whereas the tests at the loan level above test the pricing implications of low demand. These regressions take the following form: Market share j , t = % banks with RPE vs. lead j , t + ϕ j + ϕ t + ε j , t . (4)

Equation (4)incorporates both lead arranger and time effects, and we cluster standard errors at the lead arranger level.

Table 4reports the results of the baseline syndicate-level regressions (Equation (1)). These results support our hypothesis: banks are less likely to join their RPE peers’ syndicated loan deals. For example, banks that name the lead in their RPE are 0.8 percentage points less likely to join the syndicate (column 1). This decline equals about 4.5% of the unconditional probability of syndicate membership (⁠=17.9%⁠). Columns 2 and 3 show that this effect is driven by banks’ choices to participate as passive investors, as opposed to the more active role of colead. Thus, havingRPE vs. lead arrangerequal to one lowers the relative likelihood of a bank’s passive participation by about 7.1% (⁠=−0.007/0.099), and has no impact on a bank’s likelihood to act as a colead.

There are several explanations for the nonresult forColead member(column 3). Borrowers in the syndicated lending market often have several relationship banks, and they typically want these banks involved in the loan syndication process (as either lead or colead). Informal discussions with bankers suggest that the choice of which banks act as colead(s) often reflects the borrower’s desires more than those of the lead arranger. And the relationship banks are themselves eager to act as colead (if they are not the lead) to strengthen their business ties to the borrower, including future lending. As a result, both lead and colead outcomes are highly persistent.Table 4reports that past lead arrangers are almost 10 percentage points more likely to be a colead on a given loan. The reverse is also true: past coleads are more likely to be chosen as the lead arranger (untabulated). These patterns reflect the fact that many borrowers will split their business across their relationship lenders over time. Moreover, although DealScan does not allow us to measure fee splitting, colead banks typically receive a greater share of fees than passive participants. Thus, both the monetary and relationship-strengthening incentives outweigh the incentives from RPE contracts for colead positions.

Table 5reports the same model estimated separately for lines of credit and term loans. We drop roughly 1,500 facilities, such as bridge loans, standby letters of credit, or loans labeled as “other,” that do not fit into either category. Panel A shows similar sign patterns for credit lines to those inTable 4, but magnitudes increase dramatically. As inTable 4, the effect ofRPE vs. lead arrangeronly emerges forParticipant member(column 2), as opposed toColead member(column 3). The magnitude forParticipant memberis large, implying that a bank that names the lead in its RPE is 1.1 percentage points less likely to participate, which is 9.1% of the unconditional likelihood of participation (⁠=0.121for credit lines). Conversely, we find no effect ofRPE vs. lead arrangerfor term loans (panel B).

These differential results in credit lines versus term loans support our argument because only banks invest in credit lines, whereas nonbanks are the dominant investors in syndicated term loans. Whether or not one of the large banks chooses to participate in a credit line, we argue, has a meaningful effect on the lead arranger’s cost (and thus profitability) of syndication. Thus, RPE contracts make banks less eager to participate in those loans. In contrast, a bank’s decision to participate in a term loan is likely to be of little impact because the lead has many more potential nonbank investors, and banks’ term loan shares are often sold immediately after syndication.

3.2 Loan-level analysis

Table 3, panel B, reports summary statistics for our loan-level outcomes, (log of) Loan Amount, All-in Drawn Spread, Lead Arranger Share, (log of) Syndicate Size, andOther Lenders Fraction. MeanLoan Amountequals $419 million, and the meanAll-in Drawn Spread, equal to the fixed interest-rate mark up over the loan’s base rate (typically a short-term market rate, such as Libor or the Federal Funds rate), averages about 265 basis points.Lead Arranger Share, defined as the percentage of capital provided by the lead arranger at origination, averages about 25%. The average syndicate in our sample has 6.6 members, which we log transform to account for skewness. On average, 41% of syndicate members come from outside of our core top-bank sample (Other lenders fraction). Although we have data on loan amount and syndicate size for all sample loans (over 36,000), DealScan only reports the amount held by the lead arranger for a small subset of loans, so the sample forLead arranger sharecontains just over 4,000 observations (before some being partialled-out by borrower FEs), compared to nearly 33,000 loans for the sample with theAll-in drawn spread. Each sample, however, exhibits substantial heterogeneity. As before, we split the sample between credit lines and term loans as banks are the primary investors in credit lines.

Columns 1 and 2 ofTable 6report the results for thelog loan amountandAll-in drawn spread, essentially the quantity and price of the loan. The model includes year, lead arranger and borrower fixed effects (as inEquation (2)). The coefficient for% banks with RPE vs. leadin panel A is negative for quantity and positive for price (statistically significant in both cases), implying lower demand for loans arranged by banks named more frequently in the RPE of potential investors. A one-standard-deviation increase in% banks with RPE vs. leadleads to a rise in theAll-in drawn spreadof about 3.5 basis points (⁠=0.197×0.176×100⁠). This is a modest change, about 1.3% relative to the mean spread of 265 basis points. In contrast, loan size falls more, by about 6.5% (⁠=−0.37×0.176⁠). As before, these relationships are only present for credit lines; the coefficients of interest are insignificant in columns 1 and 2 of panel B. Together, these results provide evidence that lead arrangers that are more commonly cited by other banks as RPE rivals face lower demand within the credit line market, where banks are the dominant investors.

The rest ofTable 6suggests that other attributes of the syndicate shift as well, again consistent with reduced demand.Lead arranger shareincreases with% banks with RPE vs. lead, as doesOther lenders fraction(the fraction of syndicate members outside the top-15 banks, who are unaffected by RPE, as these small banks do not benchmark pay against the largest banks). Consistent with previous results, the effects are stronger for credit lines.

The analysis ofTable 6addresses a concern from our syndicate-level tests (Tables 4and5), which is that RPE contracts mention those rival banks with preexisting dependencies between the lead and the potential investor-bank, which in turn could be correlated with their syndication choices. This follows because% banks with RPE vs. leadreflects the overall choices made by banks collectively to name or not to name the lead arranger. It does not depend on the choices of the lead bank or on the choices of any individual bank as to whether to invest in any given loan. That said, it does depend on the borrower’s choice as to which bank should act as lead. This source of endogeneity would, if anything, attenuate our results because a borrower would be most apt to choose a high-RPE lead when that choice matters little to loan outcomes. To remove this effect, we report 2SLS models inTable 7, which uses the three borrower-level instruments (% banks with RPE vs. past leads,% banks with RPE vs. industry leads,% banks with RPE vs. state leads) defined above. These instrumentsdo notdepend on which bank is chosen for the loan in question; they depend on the borrower’s past lending relationships, the borrower’s industry, and the borrower’s geographical location. Each is a weighted average of the endogenous variable (% banks with RPE vs. lead), where the weights depend on past borrowing, industry, and state.

Table 7reports the first (panel A) and second stages (panel B) of the 2SLS, focusing on the sample of credit lines.27The second stage models use all three instruments. The approach passes standard diagnostic tests, as all three instruments are both individually and collectively strong. As is clear from the reported first stages, each instrument is positively and significantly correlated with% banks with RPE vs. lead, and the coefficients are all below 1.0, which makes sense because each is an average of the endogenous variable using different (predetermined) weighting schemes. Panel B shows that for all of the outcomes, we fail to reject the overidentification restrictions using Hansen’s J statistic. While this test does not rule out problems with instrument validity (ie, the exclusion restriction), it does assure estimator consistency if at least one of the instruments is valid. In our case, the only instrument that might be a concern is% banks with RPE vs. past leads, since it depends on the borrower’s past choices of lead arrangers. For the other two, it is difficult to imagine a borrower’s lending choices affecting its industry or its location (state).28

Panel B (columns 1 and 2) ofTable 7report the results for thelog loan amountandAll-in drawn spread, as well as the other syndicate structure variables. As in the OLS/fixed effects framework ofTable 6, the coefficient for% banks with RPE vs. leadis negative for quantity and positive for price (statistically significant in both cases). Magnitudes are larger in both cases, and the increase relative to OLS is economically important yet plausible. For example, a one-sigma increase in% banks with RPE vs. leadleads to a rise in theAll-in drawn spreadof about 6.0 basis points (⁠=0.339×0.176×100⁠), or about 2.3% relative to the mean spread of 265 basis points (vs. 3.5 basis point in OLS). Loan size falls about 8.6% (⁠=−0.491×0.176⁠), vs. 6.5% in OLS. We also find that lead-arranger share and the share of non-top-15 banks increase with% banks with RPE vs. lead, again with larger magnitudes than in OLS. Together, these results provide evidence that lead arrangers that are more commonly cited by other banks as RPE rivals face lower demand within the credit line market, where banks are the dominant investors.

The combined results fromTables 6and7constitute strong evidence that loan demand declines with% banks with RPE vs. lead.The evidence parallels that ofBruche, Malherbe, and Meisenzahl (2020), who show that when loan markets are “cold”—meaning loan demand is low—both loan spreads and lead-arranger share are higher. In our setting, we find that loan spreads and lead arranger share rise and loan size declines. The quantity effects are economically larger than the effects on spreads, implying a high price elasticity of capital supplied to a given borrower in this market (ie, shifting demand with% banks with RPE vs. leadidentifies supply elasticity). The estimates fromTable 7imply a price elasticity supply of about 3.7 (= 8.6%/2.3%). This high elasticity of credit supply, which holds constant everything about the borrower (recall the fixed effects) is sensible given the high degree of competition and presence of close substitutes for investors in this market.

3.3 Borrower-level analysis

The sizable response of loan size raises our next question: are borrowers worse off if their lead-arranger is named frequently by other potential investor banks? To address this, we consider the issue from the borrower’s perspective, using panel data with time and firm fixed effects. We estimate the relationship between an indicator equal to one if the firm receives a new loan and itsRPE exposure, equal to the first principal component of our three borrower-level instruments (recallEquation (3)). This strategy compresses the three measures into a single one, which capturesRPE vs. lead bankfor the “bank” likely to be selected by this borrower’s next loan (seeAppendix D).

The results inTable 8show that borrowers indeed face lower access to credit when their “natural” lead arranger is named frequently in RPE contracts by other banks. Consistent with our previous results, this relationship is strongest for credit lines. SinceRPE exposureis standardized to mean zero and unit variance, the estimate in column 4 indicates that a one-sigma increase inRPE exposurelowers the probability of receiving a new loan in a given year by 2.9%, representing a decline of 8.3% (= 0.029/0.351) relative to the sample mean.

3.5 Additional tests

We perform additional tests to corroborate the argument that the adoption of RPE contracts has intensified competition among banks and makes syndication more difficult.30First, since our syndicate-level analysis (Equation (1)) is similar to a difference-in-differences test, we conduct a pre-trend test and find no evidence that a pre-trend confounds our results (Table IA-1). Second, we show that the syndicate-level results are robust to adding RPE in the other direction, whether or not the lead arranger names the potential investor-bank as an RPE peer (Table IA-2). Third, if RPE contracts increase the competitive pressure, such pressure should be highest among peers that are ranked similarly in terms of performance. In other words, banks’ incentive to participate in their RPE rival’s loans should be lower when they are close peers, and we find exactly that (Table IA-3). Fourth, if banks choose to withhold capital to hinder their rivals’ syndication, such strategic behavior is most effective when the syndication is risky. To test this idea, we split the borrowers by their credit risk and find that our results at all four levels are driven by borrowers with low credit quality (Table IA-4). In addition, we explore the possible determinants of banks’ peer group selection and show that none of the common determinants explains within-pair variation in RPE (Table IA-5).
article.4 Conclusion:
This paper studies the competitive effects of RPE-based compensation contracts among banks in the syndicated lending market. We find that banks are less likely to join their rivals’ deals as participants, and this effect is strongest on loans with greater syndication risk. Lead arrangers respond to weak demand from rivals by issuing smaller loans with higher spreads. Rival banks’ actions raise the costs of syndication, and banks frequently targeted in RPE contracts lose market share. Borrowers are adversely affected; when the most natural choice to underwrite their loan syndicate is often named in RPE contracts, firms are less likely to receive credit lines.

Although we focus on cooperation and competition within the syndicated loan market, our results are likely exportable to other settings with similar characteristics. Banking interactions involving M&As and IPOs or, more broadly, strategic alliances and joint ventures are all potentially fruitful avenues for future research.
